learning_objectives: Distinguish counseling psychology from clinical psychology in focus and philosophy
Describe the strengths-based, developmental perspective of counseling psychology
Identify major professional roles: therapy, assessment, consultation, supervision
readings: Gelso, Williams, & Fretz, Counseling Psychology, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Counseling psychology emphasizes human strengths, developmental challenges, and person-environment fit. It focuses on normal developmental issues and intact personalities, complementing clinical psychology's emphasis on severe psychopathology.
key_concepts: Strengths-based approach: building on client assets rather than focusing solely on deficits
Vocational guidance roots and career counseling heritage
Multicultural competence as a core value of counseling psychology
Prevention and wellness promotion vs. remediation of dysfunction
Working alliance model (Bordin): goals, tasks, and bond
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two case formulations: one from a clinical perspective and one from a counseling perspective for the same client

review.value: Focuses on identifying and building upon client resources, resilience, and adaptive capacities rather than exclusively targeting pathology
Three components: agreement on therapeutic goals, agreement on tasks to achieve those goals, and the emotional bond between therapist and client

class_schedule.topic: Basic Counseling Skills: Active Listening and Empathy
learning_objectives: Demonstrate active listening skills: reflection of feeling, paraphrasing, summarizing
Distinguish between empathy, sympathy, and emotional contagion
Practice open-ended questioning and minimal encouragers

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective counseling begins with foundational helping skills. These micro-skills form the building blocks of therapeutic communication across all orientations.
key_concepts: Active listening: attending, paraphrasing, reflecting feeling, summarizing
Empathy: understanding the client's experience from their frame of reference
Open vs. closed questions and their strategic use
Minimal encouragers and silence as therapeutic tools
Nonverbal communication: congruence between words and body language
lecture_notes.demonstration: Triads exercise: one counselor, one client, one observer practicing reflection of feeling

review.value: Identifying and naming the client's emotional experience: 'It sounds like you're feeling frustrated and unheard in that relationship'
Empathy: understanding from the client's perspective. Sympathy: feeling sorry for the client. Empathy connects; sympathy creates distance

learning_objectives: Describe Holland's RIASEC model and its application in career counseling
Explain Super's life-span career development theory
Discuss modern challenges in career counseling: gig economy, career transitions, work-life balance

lecture_notes.introduction: Career counseling is a distinctive competency of counseling psychology, rooted in the field's vocational guidance origins. Career and personal issues are often intertwined.
key_concepts: Holland's RIASEC hexagon: Realistic, Investigative, Artistic, Social, Enterprising, Conventional
Person-environment fit and congruence
Super's life-span, life-space theory: growth, exploration, establishment, maintenance, disengagement
Social cognitive career theory (Lent, Brown, Hackett): self-efficacy, outcome expectations, goals
Career assessment tools: Strong Interest Inventory, Self-Directed Search
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students complete a RIASEC assessment and explore career matches using O*NET database

review.value: People and work environments can be classified into six types; satisfaction and performance are highest when person-type matches environment-type
Career choices are shaped by self-efficacy beliefs and outcome expectations, which are in turn influenced by learning experiences and contextual factors

learning_objectives: Describe the tripartite model of multicultural competence: awareness, knowledge, skills
Explain how cultural factors influence the counseling relationship and process
readings: Sue & Sue, Counseling the Culturally Diverse, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Multicultural competence is not an optional specialization but a fundamental ethical obligation for all counselors. Culture shapes every aspect of the counseling process.
key_concepts: Tripartite model: awareness of own biases, knowledge of diverse groups, skills for culturally appropriate intervention
Microaggressions in counseling: unintentional slights that undermine the alliance
Cultural formulation interview (DSM-5): structured approach to understanding cultural context
Acculturation stress and immigrant mental health
Adapting evidence-based treatments for cultural relevance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Self-reflection exercise: cultural identity exploration using the ADDRESSING framework

review.value: Hays: Age, Developmental disability, Religion, Ethnicity, Socioeconomic status, Sexual orientation, Indigenous heritage, National origin, Gender
An ongoing process of self-reflection and self-critique; recognizing the limits of one's cultural knowledge rather than claiming 'competence'

learning_objectives: Describe the stages of group development (Tuckman's model adapted for therapy groups)
Compare psychoeducational, counseling, and therapy groups
Identify therapeutic factors in groups (Yalom's curative factors)
readings: Yalom & Leszcz, The Theory and Practice of Group Psychotherapy, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Group counseling offers unique therapeutic mechanisms not available in individual therapy. It is both cost-effective and uniquely powerful for interpersonal issues.
key_concepts: Yalom's therapeutic factors: universality, altruism, instillation of hope, catharsis, interpersonal learning
Group stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning
Group leader skills: linking, blocking, modeling, process observation
Pre-group screening and informed consent
Challenges: monopolizers, silent members, scapegoating
lecture_notes.demonstration: Fishbowl exercise: inner circle conducts a brief group session while outer circle observes therapeutic factors

review.value: Discovering that others share similar struggles; among the most valued therapeutic factors for group members
The group serves as a social microcosm where members can identify and modify maladaptive interpersonal patterns in real-time

learning_objectives: Describe a structured approach to suicide risk assessment
Explain the collaborative assessment and management of suicidality (CAMS) framework
Discuss safety planning as an evidence-based intervention for suicidal ideation
readings: Jobes, Managing Suicidal Risk: A Collaborative Approach, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Suicide assessment is a core competency for all mental health professionals. Evidence-based approaches can reduce risk and save lives.
key_concepts: Suicide risk factors: prior attempts, hopelessness, social isolation, access to means
Protective factors: social connectedness, reasons for living, treatment engagement
Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) for structured assessment
CAMS framework: collaborative, ongoing assessment integrated into treatment
Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention: six-step hierarchy of coping strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play: practice conducting a suicide risk assessment using the C-SSRS

review.value: A brief intervention: (1) warning signs, (2) internal coping, (3) social contacts for distraction, (4) people to ask for help, (5) professionals/agencies, (6) means restriction
Reducing access to lethal means (firearms, medications) is one of the most effective suicide prevention strategies; many suicidal crises are brief and impulsive

questions.question: A client says: 'I got passed over for the promotion again. My boss always picks someone younger. I've been there 15 years and I just don't know what I'm doing wrong.' Write an empathic reflection of feeling and an open-ended question that would be appropriate follow-ups.
questions.answer: Reflection: 'It sounds like you're feeling both hurt and confused -- you've invested years of dedication and it feels like that's not being recognized.' Open-ended question: 'What comes up for you when you think about what this pattern means for your future at this company?' The reflection captures the emotional content (hurt, confusion) without interpretation, and the question invites deeper exploration without leading the client.

rubric.key: Avoids advice-giving, interpreting, or closed questions

questions.question: Using Holland's RIASEC model, a client scores highest on Social and Artistic types. Describe what this profile suggests about career fit, and identify two specific occupations that would be congruent.
questions.answer: The SA (Social-Artistic) profile suggests the client thrives in roles involving helping others (Social: cooperative, empathic, interpersonal) combined with creative expression (Artistic: expressive, original, nonconforming). Congruent occupations include: (1) Art therapist -- combines creative expression with therapeutic helping; (2) Music teacher -- blends artistic skills with educating and nurturing others. Other strong matches: drama therapist, creative writing instructor, counselor in arts-based programs.

rubric.key: Identifies two congruent occupations with justification

questions.question: Describe the six steps of the Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention in order, and explain why it is considered more effective than a no-suicide contract.
questions.answer: The six steps: (1) Recognize personal warning signs for a suicidal crisis, (2) Use internal coping strategies (distraction, self-soothing), (3) Contact people or social settings for distraction (not about the crisis), (4) Contact specific people who can help (trusted friends/family), (5) Contact professionals or agencies (therapist, crisis line, 988), (6) Make the environment safe by restricting access to lethal means. Safety planning is more effective than no-suicide contracts because: it is collaborative rather than coercive, it provides concrete behavioral steps, it has empirical support from randomized trials, and no-suicide contracts have no evidence of effectiveness and may create a false sense of security.

rubric.key: Lists all six steps in correct order
Explains why safety planning outperforms no-suicide contracts

questions.question: A counselor working with a Latinx immigrant client notices the client avoids eye contact and defers to the counselor's authority. Describe two potential cultural explanations and explain how cultural humility would guide the counselor's response.
questions.answer: Cultural explanations: (1) In many Latin American cultures, avoiding direct eye contact with authority figures demonstrates respeto (respect), not avoidance or disengagement. (2) Deferring to the counselor may reflect personalismo (valuing warm personal relationships with authority) and the expectation that the counselor, as an expert, should provide direct guidance. Cultural humility response: The counselor should avoid pathologizing the behavior (e.g., interpreting it as social anxiety or passivity). Instead, they should explore the client's cultural expectations for the counseling relationship, ask how the client prefers to engage, and adapt their style accordingly -- perhaps offering more direct guidance initially while building the relationship.

rubric.key: Identifies two valid cultural explanations with relevant concepts
Demonstrates cultural humility rather than pathologizing

major_assignments.title: Counseling Skills Demonstration and Self-Reflection
major_assignments.description: Conduct a 30-minute practice counseling session with a peer (recorded with consent). Submit the recording along with a detailed self-analysis evaluating your use of counseling micro-skills, identifying strengths and growth areas, and connecting your practice to counseling theory.
objectives: Demonstrate foundational counseling skills in a practice session
Critically evaluate own counseling performance using specific criteria
Connect counseling practice to theoretical frameworks
Develop a personalized professional development plan
deliverables: 2,500-word self-analysis paper with timestamped skill examples
Counseling skills rating form (self and peer)
Professional development plan with specific goals

criteria: Effective use of active listening skills
Honest and specific self-evaluation with timestamped examples
Identifies patterns in own counseling style
Recognizes both strengths and growth areas
Specific, measurable goals for skill development
Effective demonstration of foundational counseling skills
Insightful self-analysis with specific timestamped examples

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of counseling theories and techniques

skills_practice_labs.description: Ongoing evaluation of counseling skills through practice sessions

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering counseling foundations and theories

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with case-based counseling scenarios

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all skills practice labs. Missed labs cannot be made up due to the experiential nature of the work.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Recorded sessions must be submitted on time for peer review scheduling.
course_policies.collaboration: Skills practice requires partner participation. Written self-analyses must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Confidentiality of practice session content must be maintained by all participants. All written work must be original.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Video recording capability for practice sessions, access to career assessment tools, and university library databases.
